article.source: RocketNews | Top News Stories From Around the Globe
article.body_preview: Dhaka, Bangladesh - Spice trader Mohammed Enayet Ullah has made at least four attempts since November to open a letter of credit to pay for imports of cumin, cardamom and cloves, some of the most essential spices used in Bangladeshi cooking, only to be refused by banks due to a shortage of dollars.Importers in Bangladesh need to open letters of credit with one of the country's 61 scheduled banks to buy foreign goods and services. It is essentially a financial contract issued by an importer's ban

llm_connections.evidence_quote: only to be refused by banks due to a shortage of dollars
llm_connections.scope_justification: The article discusses importers being unable to open letters of credit for foreign goods and services, which directly concerns import availability at the country-wide import-financing level in the feature definition.

verification.quote_best_match: only to be refused by banks due to a shortage of
verification.judge_reasoning: The quoted wording appears in the article almost verbatim: Enayet Ullah’s attempts were 'refused by banks due to a shortage of dollars.' The text explicitly links the dollar shortage to banks refusing letters of credit, which blocks import financing, and it discusses this as a Bangladesh-wide import-financing problem rather than a single isolated case.
